text: decent economic theory, especially any decent political economy, must take its departure from the in-between of negotiated value, and from the in-betweenness of human Liminality and the Modern 222 passions. The volatile ups and downs of current economics cannot be reduced to shortcomings in the institutional setting up of market forces; they are examples of imitative behaviour that is driven by passion and emotion – greed in the bubbles, fear in the crashes. Individual investors magnetically join the crowd of others in a rush to get in or out of the market. Human beings are imitative creatures; this is even more the case once we live under the illusion that only children and animals imitate, whereas mature adults – in the best of Kantian traditions – exercise autonomous reason. Human beings, adults included, do imitate. In the contemporary situation, with processes of imitation spreading throughout the social body and on a global scale, connected to the viral networks of the internet, we can no longer afford to forget about Tarde. Imitation does not have to pass through one’s consciousness; in actual fact, Tarde argued, it happens even more smoothly when people do not recognize the fact that they are actually imitating somebody else (Tarde 1903: 77). The somnambulist follows ideas suggested by others, but believes that these are spontaneous and his own (ibid.). The possibility of illusion only increases with what Tarde calls the rise of civilization and increasing urban density – and which we can in many ways translate to our current ‘globalization’. This is because civilization (globalization) diminishes distance between people and increases the frequency of their interaction, thus renders them even more prone to imitation. A particularly clear example of such illusion concerns again the (neoclassical) economic axiom that human beings exert ‘rational choice’. However, in actual fact it is quite evident that the smooth activity of the market is also secured by the media and advertising, working through images and imitation. The short-sightedness of modern economic theory in this regard is truly astonishing, and Tarde is absolutely right in inserting a perplexed question in brackets: ‘How was it possible, then, for economists to dream of formulating theories of value in which the idea of imitation had no part?’ (ibid.: 16). Home and Dwelling O how amiable are thy dwellings, thou Lord of hosts! (Book of Psalms, 84) From an anthropological and sociological perspective, the most intriguing (and frightening) aspect of the history of economic bubbles is the fact that the current bubble-produced crisis emanated from within the most intimate and sacred place we know of: our homes. We need to understand this extremely well: the deregulation and generalized de-framing that took place from the neoliberal triumph of the 1990s very quickly targeted for speculation the most ‘real’ and substantial aspect of humanity: our dwelling practices. The housing bubble which brought the global economy to its knees was anticipated by The Economist in an article with the title Worldwide rise in house prices is the biggest bubble in history. The economic crisis that hit the world from 2008 emanated from speculation on the By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 223 value of people’s homes and houses. This amounts to nothing less than liminality erupting from within the core of primary sociability. From 2008, the housing market entered a crisis in many parts of the world. We need to be extremely careful again here, and get behind the understanding of the word ‘crisis’. From the prevailing economic rationality, the crisis is connected to the fact that prices go down, and that the market is ‘stuck’. The fact that enormous numbers of people and families have literally lost their homes should and must not be neglected, but in economic terms, this is not exactly where the nature of the crisis resides. When the market is stuck, it means that fewer people are selling and fewer people are buying. In other words, people stay where they are, and keep living in their houses, and less money is generated. The very fact that this is automatically seen as a crisis is the real crisis, a clear indication that contemporary culture is problematically oikophobic at its core. But this is the rationality, and governments constantly run behind it as they desperately try to introduce reforms that will inject new energy into the housing market. The Girardian mimetics of desire has taken as its object the house and thrown the realness of real estate into the thin air of the unreal. Some other seeming trivialities start to assume their full significance when seen in this light. During the last two decades, all major dailies in most of the Western world (but not only) increasingly started to include inserts on ‘Housing’ or ‘Home’. They quickly grew in size and in many cases became a whole paper within the paper. 3 They are always inserted at weekends, when newspapers lie around the home and when we have time to read them; when we have time to fantasize; when we are, in fact, dwelling. The inserts are free, as their production costs are financed by the real estate business. Very often, the commercial adverts for houses for sale are preceded by a series of articles, full of images, on architecture, design and ‘living’, fancy new objects which we did not know that we needed until we saw them pictured. It is increasingly unclear whether the articles are written by sales persons or journalists specialized in fashion and living – and sometimes they are actually written as a joint production of the two. Typically, in such inserts, a ‘house of the week’ is presented, with selected images and an interview with the owner. Just as people dreamed of becoming ‘worker of the month’ under communism or in American-style corporate business, we can now dream of creating the perfect house of the week and make it to the news. The real estate market thrives on the unreal, or rather, it thrives on eliminating the very boundary of
